Title: .tres is truth, JSON is cache
Summary: The Godot .tres files are canonical. Bleepforge's JSON is a derived cache that gets rebuilt from .tres on every server start.
Body:
## The model

For the seven hardcoded game domains (Dialogs, Quests, Items, Karma, NPCs, Factions, Balloons):

- The Godot `.tres` files in your project are canonical. Anything that ships is what is in the Godot project.
- Bleepforge's JSON cache under `data/<domain>/` is derived. It is rebuilt from `.tres` on every server start, kept in sync afterward by the live watcher, and pushed back to `.tres` on every save you make in Bleepforge.

## What this means in practice

- You can hand edit `.tres` in Godot and Bleepforge will catch up via the watcher within a few hundred ms.
- You can save in Bleepforge and the change shows up in Godot on the next focus.
- You should not hand edit the JSON cache. It is rebuilt on every server start and your changes will be silently overwritten.

## Why JSON exists at all

It is the pivot format that lets the rest of Bleepforge be simple. The client does not parse `.tres`; it talks JSON to the server. The catalog hook (`useCatalog`) loads JSON, not `.tres`. The integrity check runs on JSON. The catalog refresh after a save flushes the JSON cache, not the `.tres`.

JSON is also committed to git as a redundant safety net so historical states are queryable from either side. The git history of `data/items/` is human readable in a way the `.tres` history is not.

## What is not cached

Concept, Preferences, Codex, and Help are Bleepforge only. They have no `.tres` counterpart and do not participate in the reconcile or watcher pipelines. They are authoritative as JSON.
